nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Ayam Masak Paprik as a composite dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore stir-fry dish profiles and IMR Malaysia MyFCD chicken dish entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked chicken and mixed vegetables. Input values from the enriched JSON (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) are retained where consistent with these approved sources. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.5 g per 100 g based on typical cooking oil and chicken skin contribution per USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on mixed stir-fry dish with chicken and vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Masak Paprik. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on mixed protein-and-vegetable stir-fry dishes with low refined-carbohydrate content, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar Thai-style stir-fry dishes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 14.5) / 100 = 5.8 (carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 5.8 × 2.5 = 14.5 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg per 100 g) from oyster sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced oyster sauce and low-sodium soy sauce when ordering; a 250 g serving contributes approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing over 45% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40), high protein (12.2 g per 100 g), and moderate dietary fibre from mixed vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ayam Masak Paprik is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with a small portion of brown rice; the high protein and fibre content attenuate postprandial glucose response, but sugar content in chili paste and oyster sauce should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (420 mg per 100 g) and moderate fat (8.5 g per 100 g) including saturated fat from cooking oil and chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size, choose skinless chicken breast, use a minimal amount of heart-healthy oil (e.g., canola or olive oil), and substitute low-sodium soy sauce to reduce overall sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in antioxidant vegetables and anti-inflammatory compounds from kaffir lime leaves and chilies
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ayam Masak Paprik is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; use anti-inflammatory oils such as olive or canola oil and avoid refined vegetable oils to maximise the anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Masak Paprik is a Thai-inspired Malay stir-fry dish featuring bite-sized chicken pieces cooked with a vibrant medley of crunchy vegetables including cauliflower, carrots, long beans, and baby corn. The name derives from the Thai 'Pad Prik', meaning stir-fried with chilies. The sauce is a bold, savory, spicy, and slightly tangy combination of chili paste (sambal oelek or Thai chili jam), oyster sauce, and soy sauce, with the distinctive aromatic note of fresh kaffir lime leaves (daun limau purut) providing a bright citrusy fragrance. The dish is cooked quickly over high heat, preserving the crunch of the vegetables and the tenderness of the chicken. Per 100 g it provides approximately 145 kcal, 12.2 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and 5.8 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-rich, relatively low-carbohydrate main dish. Sodium is elevated at 420 mg per 100 g due to the sauce components. It has a low glycemic index (~40) and is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portioned appropriately. The dish is a staple at Malaysian warung stalls and is typically served with steamed white rice or brown rice. It is halal and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is Ayam Masak Paprik suitable for diabetics?
What vegetables are in Ayam Masak Paprik?
Is Ayam Masak Paprik the same as Gai Pad Prik?
meal_context: served with steamed white rice or brown rice

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore stir-fry dish profiles and IMR Malaysia MyFCD chicken dish entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No single approved-source entry exists for Ayam Masak Paprik as a named composite dish; values are consistent with the ai_generated input (confidence 0.9) and approved-source analogues. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and moisture data for Ayam Masak Paprik as a composite stir-fry dish are not individually reported in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024; these fields cannot be reliably disaggregated from available approved-source data for this mixed dish. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 2.5 g per 100 g based on typical cooking oil and chicken skin contribution per USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken and vegetable oil entries. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database mixed stir-fry dish profiles; no direct GI measurement for Ayam Masak Paprik exists in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per serving (250 g) calculated as (40 × 14.5) / 100 = 5.8. Tamil and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a Malay/Thai-origin dish, not a Singaporean dish requiring full Singaporean multilingual coverage; fields left null accordingly. Vietnamese, Korean, Japanese, and Indonesian local names are not applicable for this cuisine category.
